NKJV.1: What then shall we say that Abraham our father has found according to the flesh?
NKJV.2: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not before God.
NKJV.3: For what does the Scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness.”
NKJV.4: Now to him who works, the wages are not counted as grace but as debt.
NKJV.5: But to him who does not work but believes on Him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is accounted for righteousness,
NKJV.6: just as David also describes the blessedness of the man to whom God imputes righteousness apart from works:
NKJV.7: “Blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven, And whose sins are covered;
NKJV.8: Blessed is the man to whom the LORD shall not impute sin.”
NKJV.9: Does this blessedness then come upon the circumcised only, or upon the uncircumcised also? For we say that faith was accounted to Abraham for righteousness.
NKJV.10: How then was it accounted? While he was circumcised, or uncircumcised? Not while circumcised, but while uncircumcised.
NKJV.11: And he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had while still uncircumcised, that he might be the father of all those who believe, though they are uncircumcised, that righteousness might be imputed to them also,
NKJV.12: and the father of circumcision to those who not only are of the circumcision, but who also walk in the steps of the faith which our father Abraham had while still uncircumcised.
NKJV.13: For the promise that he would be the heir of the world was not to Abraham or to his seed through the law, but through the righteousness of faith.
NKJV.14: For if those who are of the law are heirs, faith is made void and the promise made of no effect,
NKJV.15: because the law brings about wrath; for where there is no law there is no transgression.
NKJV.16: Therefore it is of faith that it might be according to grace, so that the promise might be sure to all the seed, not only to those who are of the law, but also to those who are of the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all
NKJV.17: (as it is written, “I have made you a father of many nations”) in the presence of Him whom he believed—God, who gives life to the dead and calls those things which do not exist as though they did;
NKJV.18: who, contrary to hope, in hope believed, so that he became the father of many nations, according to what was spoken, “So shall your descendants be.”
NKJV.19: And not being weak in faith, he did not consider his own body, already dead (since he was about a hundred years old), and the deadness of Sarah’s womb.
NKJV.20: He did not waver at the promise of God through unbelief, but was strengthened in faith, giving glory to God,
NKJV.21: and being fully convinced that what He had promised He was also able to perform.
NKJV.22: And therefore “it was accounted to him for righteousness.”
NKJV.23: Now it was not written for his sake alone that it was imputed to him,
NKJV.24: but also for us. It shall be imputed to us who believe in Him who raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead,
NKJV.25: who was delivered up because of our offenses, and was raised because of our justification.

ESV.1: What then shall we say was gained by Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh?
ESV.2: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not before God.
ESV.3: For what does the Scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness.”
ESV.4: Now to the one who works, his wages are not counted as a gift but as his due.
ESV.5: And to the one who does not work but believes in  him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted as righteousness,
ESV.6: just as David also speaks of the blessing of the one to whom God counts righteousness apart from works:
ESV.7: “Blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven, and whose sins are covered;
ESV.8: blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin.”
ESV.9: Is this blessing then only for the circumcised, or also for the uncircumcised? For we say that faith was counted to Abraham as righteousness.
ESV.10: How then was it counted to him? Was it before or after he had been circumcised? It was not after, but before he was circumcised.
ESV.11: He received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised. The purpose was to make him the father of all who believe without being circumcised, so that righteousness would be counted to them as well,
ESV.12: and to make him the father of the circumcised who are not merely circumcised but who also walk in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham had before he was circumcised.
ESV.13: For the promise to Abraham and his offspring that he would be heir of the world did not come through the law but through the righteousness of faith.
ESV.14: For if it is the adherents of the law who are to be the heirs, faith is null and the promise is void.
ESV.15: For the law brings wrath, but where there is no law there is no transgression.
ESV.16: That is why it depends on faith, in order that the promise may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all his offspring — not only to the adherent of the law but also to the one who shares the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all,
ESV.17: as it is written, “I have made you the father of many nations” — in the presence of the God in whom he believed, who gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist.
ESV.18: In hope he believed against hope, that he should become the father of many nations, as he had been told, “So shall your offspring be.”
ESV.19: He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body, which was as good as dead (since he was about a hundred years old), or when he considered the barrenness  of Sarah's womb.
ESV.20: No unbelief made him waver concerning the promise of God, but he grew strong in his faith as he gave glory to God,
ESV.21: fully convinced that God was able to do what he had promised.
ESV.22: That is why his faith was “counted to him as righteousness.”
ESV.23: But the words “it was counted to him” were not written for his sake alone,
ESV.24: but for ours also. It will be counted to us who believe in him who raised from the dead Jesus our Lord,
ESV.25: who was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification.

NASB.1: What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, has found?
NASB.2: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not before God.
NASB.3: For what does the Scripture say? 'ABRAHAM BELIEVED GOD, AND IT WAS CREDITED TO HIM AS RIGHTEOUSNESS.'
NASB.4: Now to the one who works, his wage is not credited as a favor, but as what is due.
NASB.5: But to the one who does not work, but believes in Him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is credited as righteousness,
NASB.6: just as David also speaks of the blessing on the man to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
NASB.7: 'BLESSED ARE THOSE WHOSE LAWLESS DEEDS HAVE BEEN FORGIVEN, AND WHOSE SINS HAVE BEEN COVERED.
NASB.8: 'BLESSED IS THE MAN WHOSE SIN THE LORD WILL NOT TAKE INTO ACCOUNT.'
NASB.9: Is this blessing then on the circumcised, or on the uncircumcised also? For we say, 'FAITH WAS CREDITED TO ABRAHAM AS RIGHTEOUSNESS.'
NASB.10: How then was it credited? While he was circumcised, or uncircumcised? Not while circumcised, but while uncircumcised;
NASB.11: and he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had while uncircumcised, so that he might be the father of all who believe without being circumcised, that righteousness might be credited to them,
NASB.12: and the father of circumcision to those who not only are of the circumcision, but who also follow in the steps of the faith of our father Abraham which he had while uncircumcised.
NASB.13: For the promise to Abraham or to his descendants that he would be heir of the world was not through the Law, but through the righteousness of faith.
NASB.14: For if those who are of the Law are heirs, faith is made void and the promise is nullified;
NASB.15: for the Law brings about wrath, but where there is no law, there also is no violation.
NASB.16: For this reason [it is] by faith, in order that [it may be] in accordance with grace, so that the promise will be guaranteed to all the descendants, not only to those who are of the Law, but also to those who are of the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all,
NASB.17: (as it is written, 'A FATHER OF MANY NATIONS HAVE I MADE YOU') in the presence of Him whom he believed, [even] God, who gives life to the dead and calls into being that which does not exist.
NASB.18: In hope against hope he believed, so that he might become a father of many nations according to that which had been spoken, 'SO SHALL YOUR DESCENDANTS BE.'
NASB.19: Without becoming weak in faith he contemplated his own body, now as good as dead since he was about a hundred years old, and the deadness of Sarah's womb;
NASB.20: yet, with respect to the promise of God, he did not waver in unbelief but grew strong in faith, giving glory to God,
NASB.21: and being fully assured that what God had promised, He was able also to perform.
NASB.22: Therefore IT WAS ALSO CREDITED TO HIM AS RIGHTEOUSNESS.
NASB.23: Now not for his sake only was it written that it was credited to him,
NASB.24: but for our sake also, to whom it will be credited, as those who believe in Him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead,
NASB.25: [He] who was delivered over because of our transgressions, and was raised because of our justification.

NLT.1: Abraham was, humanly speaking, the founder of our Jewish nation. What did he discover about being made right with God?
NLT.2: If his good deeds had made him acceptable to God, he would have had something to boast about. But that was not God’s way.
NLT.3: For the Scriptures tell us, “Abraham believed God, and God counted him as righteous because of his faith.”
NLT.4: When people work, their wages are not a gift, but something they have earned.
NLT.5: But people are counted as righteous, not because of their work, but because of their faith in God who forgives sinners.
NLT.6: David also spoke of this when he described the happiness of those who are declared righteous without working for it:
NLT.7:
“Oh, what joy for those
whose disobedience is forgiven,
whose sins are put out of sight.
NLT.8:
Yes, what joy for those
whose record the LORD has cleared of sin.”
NLT.9: Now, is this blessing only for the Jews, or is it also for uncircumcised Gentiles? Well, we have been saying that Abraham was counted as righteous by God because of his faith.
NLT.10: But how did this happen? Was he counted as righteous only after he was circumcised, or was it before he was circumcised? Clearly, God accepted Abraham before he was circumcised!
NLT.11: Circumcision was a sign that Abraham already had faith and that God had already accepted him and declared him to be righteous — even before he was circumcised. So Abraham is the spiritual father of those who have faith but have not been circumcised. They are counted as righteous because of their faith.
NLT.12: And Abraham is also the spiritual father of those who have been circumcised, but only if they have the same kind of faith Abraham had before he was circumcised.
NLT.13: Clearly, God’s promise to give the whole earth to Abraham and his descendants was based not on his obedience to God’s law, but on a right relationship with God that comes by faith.
NLT.14: If God’s promise is only for those who obey the law, then faith is not necessary and the promise is pointless.
NLT.15: For the law always brings punishment on those who try to obey it. (The only way to avoid breaking the law is to have no law to break!)
NLT.16: So the promise is received by faith. It is given as a free gift. And we are all certain to receive it, whether or not we live according to the law of Moses, if we have faith like Abraham’s. For Abraham is the father of all who believe.
NLT.17: That is what the Scriptures mean when God told him, “I have made you the father of many nations.” This happened because Abraham believed in the God who brings the dead back to life and who creates new things out of nothing.
NLT.18: Even when there was no reason for hope, Abraham kept hoping — believing that he would become the father of many nations. For God had said to him, “That’s how many descendants you will have!”
NLT.19: And Abraham’s faith did not weaken, even though, at about 100 years of age, he figured his body was as good as dead — and so was Sarah’s womb.
NLT.20: Abraham never wavered in believing God’s promise. In fact, his faith grew stronger, and in this he brought glory to God.
NLT.21: He was fully convinced that God is able to do whatever he promises.
NLT.22: And because of Abraham’s faith, God counted him as righteous.
NLT.23: And when God counted him as righteous, it wasn’t just for Abraham’s benefit. It was recorded
NLT.24: for our benefit, too, assuring us that God will also count us as righteous if we believe in him, the one who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead.
NLT.25: He was handed over to die because of our sins, and he was raised to life to make us right with God.

WEB.1: What then will we say that Abraham, our forefather, has found according to the flesh?
WEB.2: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not toward God.
WEB.3: For what does the Scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was accounted to him for righteousness.”
WEB.4: Now to him who works, the reward is not counted as grace, but as something owed.
WEB.5: But to him who doesn’t work, but believes in him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is accounted for righteousness.
WEB.6: Even as David also pronounces blessing on the man to whom God counts righteousness apart from works,
WEB.7: “Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven, whose sins are covered.
WEB.8: Blessed is the man whom the Lord will by no means charge with sin.”
WEB.9: Is this blessing then pronounced on the circumcised, or on the uncircumcised also? For we say that faith was accounted to Abraham for righteousness.
WEB.10: How then was it counted? When he was in circumcision, or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision.
WEB.11: He received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had while he was in uncircumcision, that he might be the father of all those who believe, though they might be in uncircumcision, that righteousness might also be accounted to them.
WEB.12: He is the father of circumcision to those who not only are of the circumcision, but who also walk in the steps of that faith of our father Abraham, which he had in uncircumcision.
WEB.13: For the promise to Abraham and to his offspring  that he should be heir of the world wasn’t through the law, but through the righteousness of faith.
WEB.14: For if those who are of the law are heirs, faith is made void, and the promise is made of no effect.
WEB.15: For the law produces wrath, for where there is no law, neither is there disobedience.
WEB.16: For this cause it is of faith, that it may be according to grace, to the end that the promise may be sure to all the offspring,  not to that only which is of the law, but to that also which is of the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all.
WEB.17: As it is written, “I have made you a father of many nations.”  This is in the presence of him whom he believed: God, who gives life to the dead, and calls the things that are not, as though they were.
WEB.18: Besides hope, Abraham in hope believed, to the end that he might become a father of many nations, according to that which had been spoken, “So will your offspring  be.”
WEB.19: Without being weakened in faith, he didn’t consider his own body, already having been worn out, (he being about a hundred years old), and the deadness of Sarah’s womb.
WEB.20: Yet, looking to the promise of God, he didn’t waver through unbelief, but grew strong through faith, giving glory to God,
WEB.21: and being fully assured that what he had promised, he was also able to perform.
WEB.22: Therefore it also was “credited to him for righteousness.”
WEB.23: Now it was not written that it was accounted to him for his sake alone,
WEB.24: but for our sake also, to whom it will be accounted, who believe in him who raised Jesus, our Lord, from the dead,
WEB.25: who was delivered up for our trespasses, and was raised for our justification.

CSB17.1: What then will we say that Abraham, our forefather <sup>ⓒ</sup> according to the flesh, has found? <sup>[1]</sup>
CSB17.2: If Abraham was justified <sup>[2]</sup> by works, <sup>ⓓ</sup> he has something to boast about — but not before God.
CSB17.3: For what does the Scripture say? Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him for righteousness. [3] ⓔ
CSB17.4: Now to the one who works, <sup>ⓕ</sup> pay is not credited as a gift, but as something owed.
CSB17.5: But to the one who does not work, but believes on him who declares the ungodly to be righteous, <sup>ⓖ</sup> his faith is credited for righteousness.
CSB17.6: Just as David also speaks of the blessing of the person to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
CSB17.7:
Blessed are those whose lawless acts are forgiven
and whose sins are covered.
CSB17.8:
Blessed is the person
the Lord will never charge with sin. [4] ⓗ
CSB17.9: Is this blessing only for the circumcised, ⓘ then? Or is it also for the uncircumcised? For we say, Faith was credited to Abraham for righteousness. [5] ⓙ
CSB17.10: In what way then was it credited — while he was circumcised, or uncircumcised? It was not while he was circumcised, but uncircumcised.
CSB17.11: And he received the sign of circumcision <sup>ⓚ</sup> as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith <sup>[6]</sup> <sup>ⓛ</sup> while still uncircumcised. This was to make him the father <sup>ⓜ</sup> of all who believe <sup>ⓝ</sup> but are not circumcised, so that righteousness may be credited to them also.
CSB17.12: And he became the father of the circumcised, who are not only circumcised but who also follow in the footsteps of the faith our father Abraham had while he was still uncircumcised.
CSB17.13: For the promise to Abraham <sup>ⓐ</sup> or to his descendants that he would inherit the world <sup>ⓑ</sup> was not through the law, but through the righteousness that comes by faith.
CSB17.14: If those who are of the law are heirs, <sup>ⓒ</sup> faith is made empty and the promise nullified,
CSB17.15: because the law produces wrath. <sup>ⓓ</sup> And where there is no law, <sup>ⓔ</sup> there is no transgression.
CSB17.16: This is why the promise is by faith, so that it may be according to grace, <sup>ⓕ</sup> to guarantee it to all the descendants <sup>ⓖ</sup>  — not only to those who are of the law <sup>[7]</sup> but also to those who are of Abraham’s faith. He is the father of us all.
CSB17.17: As it is written: I have made you the father of many nations. [8] ⓗ He is our father in God’s sight, in whom Abraham believed — the God who gives life to the dead ⓘ and calls ⓙ things into existence that do not exist. ⓚ
CSB17.18: He believed, hoping against hope, so that he became the father of many nations [9] ⓛ according to what had been spoken: So will your descendants be. ⓜ ⓝ
CSB17.19: He did not weaken in faith when he considered <sup>[10]</sup> his own body to be already dead <sup>ⓐ</sup> (since he was about a hundred years old) <sup>ⓑ</sup> and also the deadness of Sarah’s womb. <sup>ⓒ</sup>
CSB17.20: He did not waver in unbelief at God’s promise but was strengthened in his faith and gave glory to God, <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.21: because he was fully convinced <sup>ⓔ</sup> that what God had promised, he was also able to do. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.22: Therefore, it was credited to him for righteousness. [11] ⓖ
CSB17.23: Now it was credited to him [12] was not written for Abraham alone, ⓗ
CSB17.24: but also for us. It will be credited to us who believe in him <sup>ⓘ</sup> who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead. <sup>ⓙ</sup>
CSB17.25: He was delivered up for <sup>[13]</sup> our trespasses <sup>ⓚ</sup> and raised for our justification. <sup>ⓛ</sup>

AMP.1: What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather humanly speaking, has found? [Has he obtained a favored standing?]
AMP.2: For if Abraham was justified [that is, acquitted from the guilt of his sins] by works [those things he did that were good], he has something to boast about, but not before God.
AMP.3: For what does the Scripture say? "<e>Abraham believed in</e> (trusted, relied on) <e>God, and it was credited to his account as righteousness</e> (right living, right standing with God)."
AMP.4: Now to a laborer, his wages are not credited as a favor or a gift, but as an obligation [something owed to him].
AMP.5: But to the one who does not work [that is, the one who does not try to earn his salvation by doing good], but believes and completely trusts in Him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is credited to him as righteousness (right standing with God).
AMP.6: And in this same way David speaks of the blessing on the one to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
AMP.7:
"Blessed and happy and favored are those whose lawless acts have been forgiven,
And whose sins have been covered up and completely buried.
AMP.8: "Blessed and happy and favored is the man whose sin the Lord will not take into account nor charge against him."
AMP.9: Is this blessing only for the circumcised, or also for the uncircumcised? For we say, "<e>Faith was credited to Abraham as righteousness</e>."
AMP.10: How then was it credited [to him]? Was it after he had been circumcised, or before? Not after, but while [he was] uncircumcised.
AMP.11: He received the sign of circumcision, a seal or confirmation of the righteousness which he had by faith while [he was still] uncircumcised—this was so that he would be the [spiritual] father of all who believe without being circumcised—so that righteousness would be credited to them,
AMP.12: and [that he would be] the [spiritual] father of those circumcised who are not only circumcised, but who also walk in the steps of the faith of our father Abraham which he had before he was circumcised.
AMP.13: For the promise to Abraham or to his descendants that he would be heir of the world was not through [observing the requirements of] the Law, but through the righteousness of faith.
AMP.14: If those who are [followers] of the Law are [the true] heirs [of Abraham], then faith [leading to salvation] is of no effect and void, and the promise [of God] is nullified.
AMP.15: For the Law results in [God's] wrath [against sin], but where there is no law, there is no violation [of it either].
AMP.16: Therefore, [inheriting] the promise depends entirely on faith [that is, confident trust in the unseen God], in order that it may be given as an act of grace [His unmerited favor and mercy], so that the promise will be [legally] guaranteed to all the descendants [of Abraham]—not only for those [Jewish believers] who keep the Law, but also for those [Gentile believers] who share the faith of Abraham, who is the [spiritual] father of us all—
AMP.17: (as it is written [in Scripture], "I have made you a father of many nations") in the sight of Him in whom he believed, that is, God who gives life to the dead and calls into being that which does not exist.
AMP.18: In hope against hope Abraham believed that he would become a father of many nations, as he had been promised [by God]: "So [numberless] shall your descendants be."
AMP.19: Without becoming weak in faith he considered his own body, now as good as dead [for producing children] since he was about a hundred years old, and [he considered] the deadness of Sarah's womb.
AMP.20: But he did not doubt or waver in unbelief concerning the promise of God, but he grew strong and empowered by faith, giving glory to God,
AMP.21: being fully convinced that God had the power to do what He had promised.
AMP.22: Therefore his faith <e>was credited to him as righteousness</e> (right standing with God).
AMP.23: Now not for his sake alone was it written that it was credited to him,
AMP.24: but for our sake also—to whom righteousness will be credited, as those who believe in Him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead—
AMP.25: who was betrayed and crucified because of our sins, and was raised [from the dead] because of our justification [our acquittal—absolving us of all sin before God].

MSG.1: So how do we fit what we know of Abraham, our first father in the faith, into this new way of looking at things?
MSG.2: If Abraham, by what he did for God, got God to approve him, he could certainly have taken credit for it. But the story we're given is a God-story, not an Abraham-story.
MSG.3: What we read in Scripture is, "Abraham entered into what God was doing for him, and that was the turning point. He trusted God to set him right instead of trying to be right on his own."
MSG.4: If you're a hard worker and do a good job, you deserve your pay; we don't call your wages a gift.
MSG.5: But if you see that the job is too big for you, that it's something only God can do, and you trust him to do it—you could never do it for yourself no matter how hard and long you worked—well, that trusting-him-to-do-it is what gets you set right with God, by God. Sheer gift.
MSG.6: David confirms this way of looking at it, saying that the one who trusts God to do the putting-everything-right without insisting on having a say in it is one fortunate man:
MSG.7: Fortunate those whose crimes are carted off, whose sins are wiped clean from the slate.
MSG.8: Fortunate the person against whom the Lord does not keep score.
MSG.9: Do you think for a minute that this blessing is only pronounced over those of us who keep our religious ways and are circumcised? Or do you think it possible that the blessing could be given to those who never even heard of our ways, who were never brought up in the disciplines of God? We all agree, don't we, that it was by embracing what God did for him that Abraham was declared fit before God?
MSG.10: Now think: Was that declaration made before or after he was marked by the covenant rite of circumcision? That's right, before he was marked.
MSG.11: That means that he underwent circumcision as evidence and confirmation of what God had done long before to bring him into this acceptable standing with himself, an act of God he had embraced with his whole life.
MSG.12: And it means further that Abraham is father of all people who embrace what God does for them while they are still on the "outs" with God, as yet unidentified as God's, in an "uncircumcised" condition. It is precisely these people in this condition who are called "set right by God and with God"! Abraham is also, of course, father of those who have undergone the religious rite of circumcision not just because of the ritual but because they were willing to live in the risky faith-embrace of God's action for them, the way Abraham lived long before he was marked by circumcision.
MSG.13: That famous promise God gave Abraham—that he and his children would possess the earth—was not given because of something Abraham did or would do. It was based on God's decision to put everything together for him, which Abraham then entered when he believed.
MSG.14: If those who get what God gives them only get it by doing everything they are told to do and filling out all the right forms properly signed, that eliminates personal trust completely and turns the promise into an ironclad contract! That's not a holy promise; that's a business deal.
MSG.15: A contract drawn up by a hard-nosed lawyer and with plenty of fine print only makes sure that you will never be able to collect. But if there is no contract in the first place, simply a promise—and God's promise at that—you can't break it.
MSG.16: This is why the fulfillment of God's promise depends entirely on trusting God and his way, and then simply embracing him and what he does. God's promise arrives as pure gift. That's the only way everyone can be sure to get in on it, those who keep the religious traditions and those who have never heard of them. For Abraham is father of us all. He is not our racial father—that's reading the story backwards. He is our faith father.
MSG.17: We call Abraham "father" not because he got God's attention by living like a saint, but because God made something out of Abraham when he was a nobody. Isn't that what we've always read in Scripture, God saying to Abraham, "I set you up as father of many peoples"? Abraham was first named "father" and then became a father because he dared to trust God to do what only God could do: raise the dead to life, with a word make something out of nothing.
MSG.18: When everything was hopeless, Abraham believed anyway, deciding to live not on the basis of what he saw he couldn't do but on what God said he would do. And so he was made father of a multitude of peoples. God himself said to him, "You're going to have a big family, Abraham!"
MSG.19: Abraham didn't focus on his own impotence and say, "It's hopeless. This hundred-year-old body could never father a child." Nor did he survey Sarah's decades of infertility and give up.
MSG.20: He didn't tiptoe around God's promise asking cautiously skeptical questions. He plunged into the promise and came up strong, ready for God,
MSG.21: sure that God would make good on what he had said.
MSG.22: That's why it is said, "Abraham was declared fit before God by trusting God to set him right."
MSG.24: it's also us! The same thing gets said about us when we embrace and believe the One who brought Jesus to life when the conditions were equally hopeless.
MSG.25: The sacrificed Jesus made us fit for God, set us right with God.

NIV.1:
Abraham Justified by Faith
What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather, discovered in this matter?
NIV.2: If, in fact, Abraham was justified by works, he had something to boast about—but not before God.
NIV.3: What does the Scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness.”
NIV.4: Now when a man works, his wages are not credited to him as a gift, but as an obligation.
NIV.5: However, to the man who does not work but trusts God who justifies the wicked, his faith is credited as righteousness.
NIV.6: David says the same thing when he speaks of the blessedness of the man to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
NIV.7:
“Blessed are they
whose transgressions are forgiven,
whose sins are covered.
NIV.8:
Blessed is the man
whose sin the Lord will never count against him.”
NIV.9: Is this blessedness only for the circumcised, or also for the uncircumcised? We have been saying that Abraham’s faith was credited to him as righteousness.
NIV.10: Under what circumstances was it credited? Was it after he was circumcised, or before? It was not after, but before!
NIV.11: And he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised. So then, he is the father of all who believe but have not been circumcised, in order that righteousness might be credited to them.
NIV.12: And he is also the father of the circumcised who not only are circumcised but who also walk in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham had before he was circumcised.
NIV.13: It was not through law that Abraham and his offspring received the promise that he would be heir of the world, but through the righteousness that comes by faith.
NIV.14: For if those who live by law are heirs, faith has no value and the promise is worthless,
NIV.15: because law brings wrath. And where there is no law there is no transgression.
NIV.16: Therefore, the promise comes by faith, so that it may be by grace and may be guaranteed to all Abraham’s offspring—not only to those who are of the law but also to those who are of the faith of Abraham. He is the father of us all.
NIV.17: As it is written: “I have made you a father of many nations.” He is our father in the sight of God, in whom he believed—the God who gives life to the dead and calls things that are not as though they were.
NIV.18: Against all hope, Abraham in hope believed and so became the father of many nations, just as it had been said to him, “So shall your offspring be.”
NIV.19: Without weakening in his faith, he faced the fact that his body was as good as dead—since he was about a hundred years old—and that Sarah’s womb was also dead.
NIV.20: Yet he did not waver through unbelief regarding the promise of God, but was strengthened in his faith and gave glory to God,
NIV.21: being fully persuaded that God had power to do what he had promised.
NIV.22: This is why “it was credited to him as righteousness.”
NIV.23: The words “it was credited to him” were written not for him alone,
NIV.24: but also for us, to whom God will credit righteousness—for us who believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead.
NIV.25: He was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification.

NRSVCE.1: What then are we to say was gained by Abraham, our ancestor according to the flesh?
NRSVCE.2: For if Abraham was justified by works, he has something to boast about, but not before God.
NRSVCE.3: For what does the scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness.”
NRSVCE.4: Now to one who works, wages are not reckoned as a gift but as something due.
NRSVCE.5: But to one who without works trusts him who justifies the ungodly, such faith is reckoned as righteousness.
NRSVCE.6: So also David speaks of the blessedness of those to whom God reckons righteousness apart from works:
NRSVCE.7:
“Blessed are those whose iniquities are forgiven,
and whose sins are covered;
NRSVCE.8: blessed is the one against whom the Lord will not reckon sin.”
NRSVCE.9: Is this blessedness, then, pronounced only on the circumcised, or also on the uncircumcised? We say, “Faith was reckoned to Abraham as righteousness.”
NRSVCE.10: How then was it reckoned to him? Was it before or after he had been circumcised? It was not after, but before he was circumcised.
NRSVCE.11: He received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised. The purpose was to make him the ancestor of all who believe without being circumcised and who thus have righteousness reckoned to them,
NRSVCE.12: and likewise the ancestor of the circumcised who are not only circumcised but who also follow the example of the faith that our ancestor Abraham had before he was circumcised.
NRSVCE.13: For the promise that he would inherit the world did not come to Abraham or to his descendants through the law but through the righteousness of faith.
NRSVCE.14: If it is the adherents of the law who are to be the heirs, faith is null and the promise is void.
NRSVCE.15: For the law brings wrath; but where there is no law, neither is there violation.
NRSVCE.16: For this reason it depends on faith, in order that the promise may rest on grace and be guaranteed to all his descendants, not only to the adherents of the law but also to those who share the faith of Abraham (for he is the father of all of us,
NRSVCE.17: as it is written, “I have made you the father of many nations”) — in the presence of the God in whom he believed, who gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist.
NRSVCE.18: Hoping against hope, he believed that he would become “the father of many nations,” according to what was said, “So numerous shall your descendants be.”
NRSVCE.19: He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body, which was already as good as dead (for he was about a hundred years old), or when he considered the barrenness of Sarah’s womb.
NRSVCE.20: No distrust made him waver concerning the promise of God, but he grew strong in his faith as he gave glory to God,
NRSVCE.21: being fully convinced that God was able to do what he had promised.
NRSVCE.22: Therefore his faith “was reckoned to him as righteousness.”
NRSVCE.23: Now the words, “it was reckoned to him,” were written not for his sake alone,
NRSVCE.24: but for ours also. It will be reckoned to us who believe in him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead,
NRSVCE.25: who was handed over to death for our trespasses and was raised for our justification.

NET.1: What then shall we say that Abraham, our ancestor according to the flesh, has discovered regarding this matter?
NET.2: For if Abraham was declared righteous by the works of the law, he has something to boast about — but not before God.
NET.3: For what does the scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness.”
NET.4: Now to the one who works, his pay is not credited due to grace but due to obligation.
NET.5: But to the one who does not work, but believes in the one who declares the ungodly righteous, his faith is credited as righteousness.
NET.6: So even David himself speaks regarding the blessedness of the man to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
NET.7: “Blessed are those whose lawless deeds are forgiven, and whose sins are covered;
NET.8: blessed is the one against whom the Lord will never count sin.”
NET.9: Is this blessedness then for the circumcision or also for the uncircumcision? For we say, “faith was credited to Abraham as righteousness.”
NET.10: How then was it credited to him? Was he circumcised at the time, or not? No, he was not circumcised but uncircumcised!
NET.11: And he received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised, so that he would become the father of all those who believe but have never been circumcised, that they too could have righteousness credited to them.
NET.12: And he is also the father of the circumcised, who are not only circumcised, but who also walk in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham possessed when he was still uncircumcised.
NET.13: For the promise to Abraham or to his descendants that he would inherit the world was not fulfilled through the law, but through the righteousness that comes by faith.
NET.14: For if they become heirs by the law, faith is empty and the promise is nullified.
NET.15: For the law brings wrath, because where there is no law there is no transgression either.
NET.16: For this reason it is by faith so that it may be by grace, with the result that the promise may be certain to all the descendants — not only to those who are under the law, but also to those who have the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all
NET.17: (as it is written, “I have made you the father of many nations”). He is our father in the presence of God whom he believed — the God who makes the dead alive and summons the things that do not yet exist as though they already do.
NET.18: Against hope Abraham believed in hope with the result that he became the father of many nations according to the pronouncement, “so will your descendants be.”
NET.19: Without being weak in faith, he considered his own body as dead (because he was about one hundred years old) and the deadness of Sarah’s womb.
NET.20: He did not waver in unbelief about the promise of God but was strengthened in faith, giving glory to God.
NET.21: He was fully convinced that what God promised he was also able to do.
NET.22: So indeed it was credited to Abraham as righteousness.
NET.23: But the statement it was credited to him was not written only for Abraham’s sake,
NET.24: but also for our sake, to whom it will be credited, those who believe in the one who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead.
NET.25: He was given over because of our transgressions and was raised for the sake of our justification.
